heuristics.rule: If performance reviews are strong but promotions consistently go to people with political capital, you're hitting an org ceiling, not a skill gap
heuristics.condition: When you have strong reviews for multiple cycles but aren't advancing
heuristics.action: Diagnose whether the block is internal (skill) or structural (org), then act accordingly — fix skills or leave
heuristics.because: Skill gaps and org ceilings feel identical from the inside but require completely opposite responses; misdiagnosing wastes years

heuristics.rule: If you've solved the problems you were hired to solve and no new scope is available, it's time to leave
heuristics.condition: When internal growth path is genuinely exhausted — not just 'not this cycle'
heuristics.action: Switch companies rather than wait for scope that isn't coming
heuristics.because: Staying in a role with no new scope stalls your learning rate regardless of title or compensation

heuristics.rule: Optimize for scope over title at every career stage
heuristics.condition: When evaluating a role or promotion offer
heuristics.action: Ask 'what's the most important decision I'll make in the next 12 months?' before accepting; walk away if the answer isn't immediately interesting
heuristics.because: Title without scope is a career trap — it looks good externally but doesn't build the skills that make you genuinely senior in the market sense

heuristics.rule: If you can't list three genuinely hard problems you worked on in the last 12 months, your current role isn't developing you
heuristics.condition: When assessing whether to stay or leave a role
heuristics.action: Treat the inability to list three hard problems as a stronger signal than your performance review score
heuristics.because: Performance reviews measure past output; the hardness of your problems predicts future growth rate

heuristics.rule: If your manager can't describe what the next level looks like and name two people who got there, there is no defined path
heuristics.action: Don't wait for a promotion cycle — confirm the path is real before investing in it
heuristics.because: Without a defined and evidenced path, advocacy is impossible and the goalposts will move

heuristics.rule: If you're not in strategy-setting conversations after 18 months, ask why before concluding it's an org ceiling
heuristics.condition: When you feel excluded from key organizational decisions
heuristics.action: Diagnose whether exclusion reflects thin stakeholder relationships (fixable) or a structural org ceiling (leave)
heuristics.because: Mistaking thin relationships for an org ceiling causes PMs to leave problems they could have solved

heuristics.rule: The real cost of an MBA is two years of not compounding your execution track record
heuristics.condition: When considering an MBA to accelerate a PM career
heuristics.action: Compare the MBA path against two years at a great company building craft; only pursue MBA if it provides network, legitimacy signal, or domain access you can't get otherwise
heuristics.because: Internal advocacy and execution track record — not an MBA — drive promotion to Group PM or Director at tech companies

heuristics.rule: Switch companies only after you've absorbed the lessons of your current environment
heuristics.condition: When considering leaving before fully extracting value from a role
heuristics.action: Delay the switch until you can clearly articulate what you learned; if you're running from hard feedback, fix the feedback first
heuristics.because: Problems travel with you; switching before learning means you'll face the same issues without the context you had before

heuristics.rule: Find two people 5 years ahead of you — one who stayed, one who left — and ask what they'd change
heuristics.action: Use the pattern of their regrets as your primary data point over salary benchmarks
heuristics.because: Regret patterns from people ahead of you reveal trajectory consequences that present-moment thinking systematically misses

heuristics.rule: If nominal ownership is offered without the authority to say no, it's worse than no ownership
heuristics.condition: When evaluating how much real ownership a new role provides
heuristics.action: Ask specifically about the last two decisions the PM made independently, what happened when they were wrong, and what they pushed back on and won
heuristics.because: Nominal ownership trains you to feel responsible without the authority to act — a habit that is actively harmful to career development

decision_factors.why_it_matters: Learning rate predicts where you'll be in 3–5 years more than current compensation or title; a high learning rate compounds, a low one stagnates
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Ask whether you've done something in the last 6 months that surprised you — something you didn't know you could do; if you can't answer, the role isn't growing you

decision_factors.why_it_matters: Real ownership — the ability to say no and have your judgment respected — is the core mechanism by which PM skills develop; without it, you build activity habits, not impact habits
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Ask the hiring team to name two recent decisions the PM made independently and what happened when they got something wrong; vague answers mean authority is nominal

decision_factors.why_it_matters: The blast radius of your decisions, the strategic ambiguity you must resolve, and the stakeholders who depend on your judgment determine how fast you grow toward genuinely senior capability
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Compare what the most important decision you'll make in the next 12 months is; assess number of stakeholders, ambiguity level, and organizational surface area

decision_factors.factor: Skill gap vs. org ceiling diagnosis
decision_factors.why_it_matters: The correct response to a skill gap (stay and fix it) is the opposite of the correct response to an org ceiling (leave); misdiagnosis leads to years of misdirected effort
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Check if feedback is consistent across multiple managers and reviews (skill gap) vs. politically colored and inconsistent (org signal); check if scope expanded or contracted in the last 6 months

decision_factors.factor: Manager's ability and willingness to advocate
decision_factors.why_it_matters: A manager who is political, checked out, or below the influence threshold cannot advocate for you even if they want to; your promotion depends heavily on their organizational capital
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Assess whether your manager understands what you want to do next, can describe a path to it, and has the political standing to fight for you in headcount and promotion discussions

decision_factors.factor: Company trajectory alignment with industry direction
decision_factors.why_it_matters: Staying at a company moving against industry direction limits your external market value and narrows the problems you'll encounter
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Evaluate whether the company's bets are in domains growing in importance; assess whether your external network credibility is growing or stagnating

decision_factors.factor: Career stage fit with company stage
decision_factors.why_it_matters: Early-career PMs benefit from structure and craft-building at larger companies; mid-career PMs need ambiguity and high-stakes ownership that large structured environments can no longer provide
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Identify whether you need to build craft (favor process-heavy environments) or judgment under ambiguity (favor earlier-stage, higher-ownership environments)

decision_factors.why_it_matters: Switching companies to escape feedback or avoid hard situations doesn't remove the underlying skill gap — it relocates it to a new context without the institutional knowledge you had before
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Ask honestly whether the reasons for leaving are organizational (no path, no scope, political block) or personal (hard feedback, discomfort, conflict); if personal, address before leaving

decision_factors.factor: MBA as filter in target domain
decision_factors.why_it_matters: MBA is a genuine filter in some domains (consulting, PE-backed product, certain enterprise environments) and largely irrelevant in others (tech PM promotion to Director)
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Identify whether the specific role or domain you're targeting uses MBA as a real hiring or promotion filter, not just a preference signal

hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: You gain a credential that looks good externally but doesn't build the skills the market actually pays for at senior levels; the gap between your title and your capability widens silently
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: Mid-career PMs at slower-growth or structured companies who use LinkedIn visibility as a proxy for career progress

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Joining a high-brand company for the name
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: Brand companies often come with reduced decision authority, slower feedback loops, and cog-in-machine dynamics that actively slow skill development despite the resume signal
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: PMs who are mid-career and need ownership to grow, who mistake brand association for skill-building opportunity

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Pursuing an MBA to accelerate PM career
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: Two years of not compounding an execution track record; re-entry requires rebuilding credibility from scratch, and the MBA doesn't substitute for internal advocacy at tech companies
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: PMs already in product roles who are progressing, even if slowly, and use the MBA as a perceived shortcut to senior levels

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Staying in a large, structured company past the learning horizon
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: The structure that was a scaffold becomes a comfort — it removes the ambiguity and ownership pressure that would force the next level of development, and this feels like stability rather than stagnation
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: Mid-career PMs who are good at navigating structured environments and conflate comfort with growth

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Accepting nominal ownership in a role
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: You develop the habit of feeling responsible without the authority to act — a disposition that is actively harmful and hard to unlearn when real ownership becomes available
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: PMs earlier in their careers who don't yet have a calibrated baseline for what real ownership feels like

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Switching companies to avoid hard feedback
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: The skill gap travels with you to the new role, now without the institutional context and relationships you had before, making the problem harder to fix in a new environment
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: PMs who have received consistent negative feedback and interpret company-switching as a reset rather than a relocation of the problem

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Leaving before absorbing the lessons of the current environment
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: You close the learning loop prematurely and arrive at the next role without the synthesis that would have made the previous experience compound; you'll likely encounter the same situations without the pattern-matching to handle them
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: PMs who switch frequently in search of better conditions rather than extracting full value from each environment

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Waiting for scope expansion that isn't structurally available
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: Each cycle you wait reinforces the org's model of you at your current level, making internal reframing harder and costing compounding time that could be spent in a higher-scope role
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: PMs who have exhausted internal growth paths but delay leaving due to comfort, compensation, or optimism about org change

failure_patterns.pattern: Diagnosing an org ceiling as a skill gap and staying to fix something that isn't broken
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Org ceilings and skill gaps feel identical from the inside; it's cognitively easier to accept a self-improvement narrative than to conclude the environment is the problem
failure_patterns.consequence: Years of effort directed at non-issues while the actual constraint — structural politics, no available scope — remains unchanged
warning_signs: Performance reviews are consistently strong but promotions go to people with political capital
The PM role at your level hasn't changed in 2+ years despite your growth

failure_patterns.pattern: Diagnosing a skill gap as an org ceiling and leaving without fixing the underlying issue
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Switching companies feels like agency and progress; accepting hard feedback requires sustained discomfort
failure_patterns.consequence: The skill gap travels to the new role without the institutional knowledge and relationships that made it manageable before
warning_signs: Feedback about the same 2–3 things has come from multiple managers or reviews
The reason for leaving feels more like relief than strategic repositioning

failure_patterns.pattern: Staying in a large structured environment past the point where it teaches anything
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Structure is comfortable and rewards execution of known playbooks; the absence of growth is invisible because performance metrics still look good
failure_patterns.consequence: The PM exits their formative mid-career years without the ambiguity tolerance and ownership experience that the market requires at senior levels
warning_signs: Can't identify something done in the last 6 months that was genuinely surprising or difficult
The 'hardest problems' of the last year aren't actually hard when described to an outsider

failure_patterns.pattern: Optimizing for title over scope and calling it a promotion
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Title is visible and legible to the external market; scope is invisible until it becomes a gap at the next level
failure_patterns.consequence: The PM accumulates a senior title without the corresponding capability, creating a gap that becomes apparent — and damaging — at the next stage
warning_signs: Unable to clearly answer 'what's the most important decision I'll make in the next 12 months?' with something genuinely interesting
The Senior PM role involves one small feature with minimal stakeholders

failure_patterns.pattern: Mistaking exclusion from strategy conversations for an org ceiling when it's actually thin stakeholder relationships
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Exclusion from rooms feels structural and external; acknowledging that your relationships are the issue requires more personal accountability
failure_patterns.consequence: The PM leaves an environment where they could have grown by building relationships, and carries the thin-relationship pattern to the next company
warning_signs: Haven't been invited to strategy-setting conversations after 18 months
Unable to name whose opinion shapes outcomes before anything is written down

failure_patterns.pattern: Pursuing an MBA as a response to career stagnation without diagnosing the actual cause
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: The MBA question is often a proxy for 'am I advancing fast enough?' — it feels like action when the real problem is undiagnosed
failure_patterns.consequence: Two years of non-compounding execution experience, followed by re-entry where the original problem (unclear growth path, wrong environment, skill gap) still exists
warning_signs: Primary motivation is 'I don't know what else to do'
Already in a PM role and progressing, even if slowly

failure_patterns.pattern: Treating 'shipped' as the definition of success rather than 'worked'
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Shipping is measurable and creates short-term social validation; whether the thing worked is often unclear for months and requires sitting with uncertainty
failure_patterns.consequence: PMs build a habit of output-orientation rather than outcome-orientation, which becomes a ceiling at senior levels where results — not activity — are the currency
warning_signs: Success stories focus on velocity and volume of launches rather than user or business outcomes
Post-launch analysis is shallow or absent from the PM's workflow

diagnostic_questions.question: In the last 6 months, did your scope of decisions expand or contract?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether the current environment is offering growth or pulling back; contraction without explanation is an org signal, not a skill signal
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: What specifically expanded — is it more ambiguity, more stakeholders, or just more volume of the same work?
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: Was the contraction explained? If not, who owns the scope that used to be yours?

diagnostic_questions.question: Can you name 3 decisions you made in the last month that your manager doesn't know about?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether real ownership exists — decisions that don't require reporting up signal actual authority; inability to name any means the ownership is thinner than the title suggests
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: Are those decisions consequential — do they have real stakes and real feedback loops?
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: Is this because you escalate by habit or because the org expects escalation? Both are diagnostic but require different fixes

diagnostic_questions.question: What specific feedback has been consistent across more than one manager or review cycle?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Multi-source consistent feedback is almost always a real skill gap; inconsistent or politically-colored feedback is usually an org signal
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: Can you identify specific behaviors a more senior PM exhibits that you don't yet have? If yes, this is a skill gap — stay and fix it
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: Has any feedback felt arbitrary or shifted depending on political context? If yes, this is an org signal, not a skill gap

diagnostic_questions.question: If you left tomorrow, what would be missing that you haven't learned yet?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether the current role still has extractable value; if the answer is 'nothing significant,' staying is likely a comfort decision, not a growth decision
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: Is that learning actually available in the current role — is there scope and opportunity to acquire it, or is it theoretically available but structurally inaccessible?
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: Have you been honest with yourself about whether you're avoiding something rather than completing something?

diagnostic_questions.question: Does your current manager understand what you want to do next, and can they describe a path to it?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether effective advocacy is possible; a manager who can't articulate your path can't fight for you even if they want to
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: Can they name two people who have made that transition — is there evidence the path is real, not aspirational?
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: Is this because you haven't told them, or because they don't have the organizational knowledge or capital to know?

diagnostic_questions.question: When did you last surprise yourself — do something in your current role you didn't know you could do?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether the role is still at the growth edge; inability to recall a recent surprise is a strong signal the role is no longer developing new capabilities
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: Is that happening regularly, or was it a one-time event 18 months ago?
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: Is the absence of surprise because the role is too comfortable, or because external factors (org structure, limited scope) removed the conditions for growth?

diagnostic_questions.question: What are the last two big decisions the team made and why?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether real decision-making authority exists in the team and whether the reasoning is legible and substantive — used in interviews to evaluate whether a prospective role will make you better
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: Were those decisions made by the PM or by a committee — who actually had the call?
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: Vague or evasive answers signal that decisions are made elsewhere; this is a strong signal to walk away from the role

industry_realities.common_belief: Promotion to Senior PM signals genuine seniority in the market
industry_realities.actual_reality: Most companies promote to the HR definition of senior (tenure + competency signals), not the market definition (owning a problem from ambiguous to shipped with minimal oversight); the two rarely align
industry_realities.implication: Don't treat a Senior PM title as evidence that you're genuinely senior — assess your capability against the market definition, not the HR band

industry_realities.common_belief: Joining a high-brand company is good for your career development
industry_realities.actual_reality: High-brand companies often mean becoming a cog in a large machine — reduced decision authority, slower feedback loops, and narrower scope that trades development for prestige
industry_realities.implication: Evaluate roles at brand companies by the same ownership and scope criteria as any other role; brand does not substitute for development

industry_realities.common_belief: An MBA accelerates PM career progression to senior levels
industry_realities.actual_reality: For progression to Group PM or Director at tech companies, internal advocacy and execution track record drive promotion — not an MBA; the MBA is a legitimate filter only in specific domains
industry_realities.implication: Only pursue an MBA if it provides network, legitimacy signal, or domain access genuinely unavailable through two years of craft-building at a strong company

industry_realities.common_belief: Switching companies resets the clock and gives you a fresh start
industry_realities.actual_reality: Problems — skill gaps, thin relationships, avoidance of hard feedback — travel with you; a new environment removes institutional context that made the problem manageable without removing the problem
industry_realities.implication: Diagnose honestly whether you're leaving for organizational reasons or personal ones; fix personal issues before switching or you'll face them in a harder context

industry_realities.common_belief: If you have strong performance reviews, you should be getting promoted
industry_realities.actual_reality: Strong reviews without scope expansion after 18+ months often signals an org ceiling — promotions in political environments go to people with political capital, not necessarily the highest performers
industry_realities.implication: Track scope expansion alongside review scores; if scope is flat or contracting despite strong reviews, treat this as an org ceiling signal and assess whether to leave

industry_realities.common_belief: Ownership is a standard feature of PM roles — the title implies it
industry_realities.actual_reality: Nominal ownership is common and worse than no ownership; many PM roles come with the language of ownership but without the authority to say no, make independent decisions, or own consequences
industry_realities.implication: Test ownership claims during the interview process with specific behavioral questions about past decisions and consequences; don't accept ownership language without evidence

industry_realities.common_belief: Larger, more established companies are better places to develop as a PM
industry_realities.actual_reality: This is stage-dependent: large structured companies are better for early-career craft-building; mid-career PMs typically need the ambiguity, ownership, and high-stakes decisions that large structured environments can no longer provide
industry_realities.implication: Match company stage to career stage; staying in a structured environment past your learning horizon trades growth for comfort

industry_realities.common_belief: The interview process tells you whether you can do the job
industry_realities.actual_reality: The interview process is designed to evaluate whether you can do the job; the candidate must independently run a parallel process to evaluate whether the job will make them better
industry_realities.implication: Use the interview to ask about real decisions, ownership evidence, and growth path — treat poor answers as walk-away signals regardless of offer quality

junior_vs_senior_signals.junior_pattern: Treats the roadmap as immutable; focuses on executing what's there
junior_vs_senior_signals.senior_pattern: Makes the space argument — 'we shouldn't build any of these; here's the real problem' — and challenges whether the roadmap addresses the right things
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: Senior PMs create leverage by solving the right problem; junior PMs create activity by solving the assigned problem, regardless of whether it matters

junior_vs_senior_signals.junior_pattern: Seeks permission from stakeholders and managers before committing to a direction
junior_vs_senior_signals.senior_pattern: Builds conviction from user and market signals first, then brings stakeholders along
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: Permission-seeking makes you dependent on others' judgment rather than developing your own; conviction-building is the mechanism by which senior PMs create organizational trust

junior_vs_senior_signals.junior_pattern: Defines success as 'shipped' — the work is done when it's launched
junior_vs_senior_signals.senior_pattern: Defines success as 'worked' — the launch is the beginning of the feedback loop, not the end of the work
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: Output-orientation produces activity; outcome-orientation produces impact; the market pays for impact at senior levels

junior_vs_senior_signals.junior_pattern: Reacts to escalations when they arrive
junior_vs_senior_signals.senior_pattern: Prevents escalations by reading stakeholder dynamics proactively and building capital before it's needed
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: Reactive escalation handling is fire-fighting; proactive stakeholder management is a force multiplier that determines who gets resources and whose concerns make it into plans

junior_vs_senior_signals.junior_pattern: Frames every problem as a feature request — takes the problem as stated and seeks to build a solution
junior_vs_senior_signals.senior_pattern: Reframes problems upstream — questions whether the stated problem is the real problem before engaging with solutions
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: Building the wrong thing efficiently is worse than not building; senior PMs create more value by problem selection than by execution quality

junior_vs_senior_signals.junior_pattern: Treats eng and design as execution resources who implement PM decisions
junior_vs_senior_signals.senior_pattern: Treats eng and design as strategic partners whose judgment actively changes the PM's judgment
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: Teams where PM treats partners as execution resources produce worse outcomes and have higher attrition; senior PMs compound through the team's judgment, not just their own

junior_vs_senior_signals.junior_pattern: Resolves ambiguity prematurely to reduce discomfort and create the appearance of clarity
junior_vs_senior_signals.senior_pattern: Holds ambiguity without resolving it until the evidence justifies resolution; distinguishes between productive ambiguity and unclear thinking
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: Premature resolution of ambiguity produces false confidence and wrong decisions; senior PMs' tolerance for ambiguity is what allows them to make better calls with incomplete information

junior_vs_senior_signals.junior_pattern: Confuses activity with impact — measures progress by volume of work, meetings, and output
junior_vs_senior_signals.senior_pattern: Measures progress by change in outcomes — user behavior, business metrics, team capability
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: Activity is visible and socially rewarded in the short term; impact is what determines whether you get promoted and whether your career compounds
